moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Van Geet Opening, an unconventional but playable approach where we develop our knight before moving the e-pawn. This move controls d5 and e4, important central squares, while keeping flexibility about our pawn structure—we might support e4 later or pursue alternative plans. The Van Geet is less forcing than 1.e4 or 1.d4, which means Black has more ways to equalize, but it avoids well-prepared defenses and can lead to interesting middlegames where our early development gives us practical winning chances.

Black stakes a claim in the center immediately, mirroring our knight development by occupying e5 instead of responding symmetrically with 1...Nc6. This is a solid, flexible response that opens lines for Black's light-squared bishop and creates space. By playing e5, Black actually 'reverses' the typical Nimzowitsch setup (where White usually plays Nc3 after ...e5), gaining time and flexibility to respond to our next move—whether we push e4, support the center differently, or pursue a flank strategy.
